coredata.dc:title: 2D/2D g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2 Z-scheme heterojunction decorated with CQDs for enhanced Photocatalytic activity
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yueying
Chen, Jin
Yang, Xiaofeng
Liu, Xinwei
Que, Meidan
Ma, Yuzhao
Li, Yanjun
coredata.dc:description: Although the g-C3N4/TiO2 Z-scheme heterojunction has superior photocatalytic performance; however, photocatalytic efficiency is still limited by the fast recombination of photoexcited charges. Herein, we propose an electrostatic self-assembly technique to enhance the photocatalytic activity by decorating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the heterojunction. The relationships among composition, phase structure, electrochemical and catalytic performances were investigated, indicating the adhesion of CQDs. A fantastic photocatalytic capability was achieved in 80 μL CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2, and the photocatalytic activity exhibited exceptional stability and broad applicability for the degradation of rhodamine B (>99 %) and tetracycline hydrochloride (>91 %) under both ultraviolet and visible light irradiation, owing to the significant enhancement of photogenerated carrier separation by CQDs. In the photocatalytic reaction, h+ is the most essential active group through the free radical trapping experiment. This research serves as a guide for modifying photocatalysts. This study provides a promising system to promote photocatalytic efficiency, and the CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 to be used for antibiotic and organic pollutant removal on a large scale.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
(001)-TiO2
G-C3N4
Z-scheme heterojunction
Photocatalysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Independent Research and Development project of State Key Laboratory of Green Building in Western China (No. LSZZ202118 to Jin Chen), the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 21JY021 to Xiaofeng Yang), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (No. 2023-JC-YB-454 to Xiaofeng Yang), the State Key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy (Wuhan University of Science and Technology) (No. G202108 to Yanjun Li).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) (001)-TiO2, CN/T, 80 C/CN/T and (b) CQDs.
SEM images: (a) (001)-TiO2, (b) g-C3N4, (c) CN/T, (d-e) 80 C/CN/T. (f) EDX spectrum, (g-h) TEM images and (i) elemental mapping of 80 C/CN/T.
(a) FTIR spectra of g-C3N4, (001)-TiO2 and 80 C/CN/T. XPS spectra of the (b) survey, (c) C1s, (d)N1s, (e) O1s, (f) Ti2p of g-C3N4, (001)-TiO2, CN/T and 80 C/CN/T.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of (001)-TiO2, g-C3N4, CN/T, and 80 C/CN/T, (b) Calculate the bandgap from the UV-Vis DRS results. Transient photocurrent response patterns (c) and EIS (d) of (001)-TiO2, CN/T, and 80 C/CN/T.
Photo-degradation curves of RhB and first-order kinetic fitting curves of x C/CN/T under UV irradiation (a)(b) and visible light irradiation (c)(d).
Photo-degradation curves of TC-HCl and first-order kinetic fitting curves of x C/CN/T under UV irradiation (a)(b) and visible light irradiation (c)(d).
Degradation curves of (a) RhB and (b) TC-HCl over sample 80 C/CN/T for reusing four times.

(a) Experiment of capturing active species for visible light catalytic degradation of RhB by 80 C/CN/T. The photocatalytic mechanism of C/CN/T nanocomposite connected by (b) the type Ⅱ heterojunction and (c) the Z-type heterojunction.

head.ce:title: 2D/2D g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2 Z-scheme heterojunction decorated with CQDs for enhanced Photocatalytic activity

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The State Key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The State Key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: State Key Laboratory of Green Building in Western China, Xian University of Architecture & Technology, Xi'an 710055, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Green Building in Western China, Xian University of Architecture & Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although the g-C3N4/TiO2 Z-scheme heterojunction has superior photocatalytic performance; however, photocatalytic efficiency is still limited by the fast recombination of photoexcited charges. Herein, we propose an electrostatic self-assembly technique to enhance the photocatalytic activity by decorating carbon quantum dots (CQDs) onto the heterojunction. The relationships among composition, phase structure, electrochemical and catalytic performances were investigated, indicating the adhesion of CQDs. A fantastic photocatalytic capability was achieved in 80 μL CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2, and the photocatalytic activity exhibited exceptional stability and broad applicability for the degradation of rhodamine B (>99 %) and tetracycline hydrochloride (>91 %) under both ultraviolet and visible light irradiation, owing to the significant enhancement of photogenerated carrier separation by CQDs. In the photocatalytic reaction, h+ is the most essential active group through the free radical trapping experiment. This research serves as a guide for modifying photocatalysts. This study provides a promising system to promote photocatalytic efficiency, and the CQDs/g-C3N4/TiO2 to be used for antibiotic and organic pollutant removal on a large scale.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The expansion of the human population and the development of industrialized economies have exerted significant pressure on the natural systems of our earth [1]. The issue of water pollution, primarily caused by organic dyes and pigments produced by the chemical industry, has become increasingly severe [2]. Additionally, various industrial wastewaters contribute significantly to the depletion of available water resources. The wastewater must undergo thorough purification prior to utilization due to its high concentration of hazardous residues such as heavy metals [3,4], dyes [5], and pharmaceuticals [6]. These residues will harm not only human health but also the ecological environment. Photocatalysis is an effective means to treat pollutants in wastewater [7]. Among various semiconductor catalysts, especially those with strong oxidation-reduction power, which plays a pivotal role in degrading organic compounds in wastewater. Nevertheless, the photoresponse range, the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, and the movement speed of charge are all critical factors of the photocatalytic reaction process. Semiconductor catalysts that exhibit exceptional performance, stability, environmental friendliness, and affordability have garnered significant attention in both scientific and engineering communities.

The previous studies on photocatalytic technology have concluded that TiO2 is an economical, non-corrosive, and extensively investigated photocatalyst. Furthermore, the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 is directly associated with its exposed crystal surface [8]. While the (101) crystal surface of anatase TiO2 is thermodynamically stable, its energy density is just 0.44 J/m2, which has a deficiency in absorbing sunlight energy. The energy density of the (001) crystal surface of TiO2 is practically twice than that of the (101) plane (0.90 J/m2), and the catalytic performance and adsorption of TiO2 will increase with the percentage area of the (001) plane [9,10]. On the other hand, TiO2 has a limited range of optical responses, and the majority of them are in the region of ultraviolet light, which only makes up 5 % of solar energy [11]. It is another major challenge to improve the inadequate utilization of sunlight by TiO2. Numerous studies have demonstrated that the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of precious metals helps strengthen the photocatalyst effect by shifting the luminescent range to a longer wavelength and speeding up the electron transfer rate in the catalytic reaction [12–15].

However, this strategy incurs significant additional costs and does not facilitate the widespread use of TiO2. The coupling of TiO2 with other semiconductors capable of forming staggered bands appears to be a more cost-effective approach for modifying TiO2 compared to the doping of precious metals. The plentiful reports have proven that it is effective to charge separation and the interfacial charge carrier in the photocatalytic process may be optimized by combining TiO2 with a semiconductor. For instance, CuO/TiO2 [16], TiO2@Fe3O4 [17], TiO2@CdS [18], TiO2/BDD [19], ZnIn2S4/TiO2 [20], g-C3N4/TiO2 [21,22], etc. TiO2 converts into an energy platform when graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is added, and this platform may gather electrons from g-C3N4 to prolong the lifespan of photogenerated carriers [23]. Due to the narrow bandgap (2.7 eV) and visible light response capabilities of g-C3N4 [24,25], which becomes an ideal catalyst to tackle the issue of the deficient photoresponse of TiO2 in the visible light wavelength region. By increasing the specific surface area of two-dimensional g-C3N4 nanosheets, the number of reaction sites can be multiplied, while shortening the diffusion length could lower the recombination between electron and hole [24,26,27]. There have only been a few reports on the use of 2D/2D (001)-TiO2/g-C3N4 in photocatalysis, and it has been noticed [28,29] that both the photocatalytic degradation speed and hydrogen evolution rate may still have upgraded potential. Quantum dots (QDs) are a noteworthy choice as a co-catalyst to promote the catalytic ability of 2D/2D (001)-TiO2/g-C3N4. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have become a rising star in the QDs family, which rely on the adjustment of size and surface loading functional groups to make their inimitable photoluminescence (PL) tunable [30]. The exceptional properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), such as up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL) and electron transfer, have led to their application in catalysis [31–33]. These properties contribute to the photoresponse ability of CQDs and their capacity to suppress carrier recombination.

Herein, we are committed to designing a new Z-type heterojunction made of 0D CQDs-decorated g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2. The g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2 system can benefit from the addition of CQDs to increase its photocatalytic capacity, and the performance of the obtained CQDs/g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2 was examined for the photocatalytic degradation of rhodamine B and tetracycline hydrochloride under UV and visible light irradiation, respectively. The enhancement in catalytic activity can be attributed to the accelerated separation and efficient transmission of electron-hole pairs facilitated by the internal electric field generated at the highly interconnected heterojunction interfaces, which is closely associated with the synergistic electron transfer mediated by CQDs. In addition, considering the results of active species capture tests, a plausible mechanism for enhanced catalytic efficiency was proposed. This concept holds significant implications for broadening the scope of applications for g-C3N4/TiO2-based materials.

Tetrabutyl titanate (C16H36O4Ti, ≥ 98.0 %), hydrofluoric acid (HF, 40.0 %), citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O, ≥ 98.0 %), ethyl alcohol (C2H6O, 99.0 %), methyl alcohol (CH4O, ≥ 99.5 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥ 96.0 %), melamine (C3N3(NH2)3, AR), ammonia water (NH3·H2O, ≥ 90 %) and deionized (DI) water were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

TiO2 nanosheets with excellent (001) exposed facets were synthesized by a solvothermal method [34]. Typically, 17 mL of C16H36O4Ti and 4.1 mL of HF were combined gently, and the solution was then stirred for 30 min. The reaction mixture was then placed into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 24 h. Precipitates were repeatedly rinsed with a 0.1 mol/L NaOH aqueous solution, DI water, and absolute ethanol, successively to remove the residual F- in the product, after the autoclave must have been chilled to room temperature. Finally, the precipitate was dried at 60 °C in a vacuum oven.

The pure g-C3N4 is commonly fabricated by a thermal polycondensation method [35]. Typically, urea powders are heated at 550 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. After cooling, the yellow powder was collected for further use.

The CQDs solution was synthesized according to the following procedure. Especially, 3.0 g of C6H8O7·H2O was dispersed in 45 mL of DI water, and NH3·H2O was added dropwise to make the solution neutral. The blended liquid was then poured into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated for 8 h at 180 °C. A yellow-brown, transparent solution was obtained after cooling to room temperature. The solution was dialyzed with a 3500 Da dialysis bag to obtain CQDs solution.

The CQDs/g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2 ternary composite was prepared by electrostatic self-assembly method, and Scheme 1 shows the preparation procedure. The g-C3N4 with a weight ratio of 3 % was placed in a beaker, and 30 mL of CH4O was added for ultrasonic dispersion for 30 min. Then, (001)-TiO2 and x μL of CQDs solutions (x = 10, 30, 50, 80, 100) were added to the mixed solution, respectively, and the mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 2 h in a fume hood, and the powder was collected after the CH4O evaporated. The obtained 0D/2D/2D CQDs/g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2 composites were referred to as x C/CN/T. For comparison, g-C3N4/(001)-TiO2 (CN/T)was synthesized by the same processes without adding CQDs solution.

X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) was performed using a Bruker D/max 2400. The microscopic morphologies were collected using FEI Quanta 250 FEG, and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was obtained on Tecnai G2 F20. The Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) was analyzed using Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20. X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out using the XPS spectrometers (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were acquired using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3010), and the reference standard is BaSO4.

The photocurrent density and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were carried out with an electrochemical analyzer (CHI 760D, China) by using a standard three-electrode system, which included a Pt wire as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode, and the prepared samples as the working electrode. A 500 W Xe arc lamp through a UV-cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm) served as a light source, and 0.1 M Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. The working electrode was made up of fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) glass with Nafion. The EIS experiment was collected at an open-circuit voltage of 5 mV.

Using a 500 W high-temperature mercury lamp and a 500 W xenon lamp (cut-off wavelength λ > 420 nm) as the UV and visible light sources. The photocatalytic performance of the catalysts was evaluated by the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC-HCl) under different light sources. 40 mg of catalyst was dispersed in 40 mL of pollutant solution (10 mg/L). Firstly, the mixture was stirred in the dark for 40 min to ensure the adsorption-desorption balance, subsequently exposed to different lights, about 2 mL of the suspension was taken at certain intervals during irradiation. After removing the catalyst from the mixture using the filter head, the absorbance of the supernatant was measured through the ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer. And the stability of the optimal-performing photocatalyst was characterized at the same time.

The XRD patterns of (001)-TiO2, CN/T, 80 C/CN/T and CQDs are presented in Fig. 1. Diffraction peaks with high-resolution located at 24.9°, 37.0°, 47.2°, 53.5°, 54.4°, 62.0°, 69.9° and 75.0°, are observed for TiO2, which means that the synthesized TiO2 is the anatase structure (JCPDS#71-1169). The as-prepared TiO2 has a sharp (200) characteristic peak, indicating that the crystal size of TiO2 in the (100)/(010) direction is large and has a (001) highly exposed face, namely (001)-TiO2. In contrast, the (004) diffraction peak broadened after the samples were combined due to the reduction in size of (001)-TiO2 [36]. In the synthesis processes of CN/T and C/CN/T, the peak intensity at (004) of the composite sample is always lower than that at (200), demonstrating that the crystal structure of (001)-TiO2 will remain unchanged [37]. As a result, the primary diffraction peaks of (001)-TiO2 (JCPDS#71-1169) are clearly visible in the XRD pattern of the composite sample. The weak strength of the diffraction peak at 2θ = 27.7° and the absence of the diffraction peak at 2θ = 13.6° in CN/T reveal the tiny amount of g-C3N4 injected. In the XRD pattern of 80 C/CN/T, the in-plane accumulation of g-C3N4 aromatic rings exhibits the (002) diffraction peak at 2θ = 27.7°. The diffraction peak width of C/CN/T is limited because of the powerful electrochemical oxidation process caused by the conversion of the Ti-O-C bonds formed between CQDs and (001)-TiO2 [38] . Besides, a characteristic peak is unique to CQDs near 2θ = 26.0° in the CQDs and C/CN/T, which proved successful synthesis of CQDs and the CQDs are firmly affixed to the CN/T surface.

The microstructures of (001)-TiO2, g-C3N4, CN/T, and 80 C/CN/T are depicted in Fig. 2. The (001)-TiO2 nanostructures appear as relatively regular, evenly sized small squares. g-C3N4 is made by stacking two-dimensional ultrathin nanosheets [39]. When (001)-TiO2 and g-C3N4 are combined (Fig. 2(c)), (001)-TiO2 almost completely covers the surface of g-C3N4. In the case of the C/CN/T system (Fig. 2(d-e)), the CQDs were adhered to the (001)-TiO2 particles and exhibited evenly spaced “little white dots”. Then, the synthesized C/CN/T sample was further subjected to EDS mapping analysis (Fig. 2(i)) to substantiate the good dispersion of CQDs on the surface of the CN/T phases, and the peak-like spectrum is shown in Fig. 2(f). Fig. 2(g-h) are TEM images of 80 C/CN/T. Initially, g-C3N4 carrying small-sized (001)-TiO2 square nanosheets is plainly visible. After enlarging the ringed region (Fig. 2(h)), many near-spherical nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 3 nm were covered on the CN/T surface, further verifying the successful adsorption of CQDs.

The surface functional groups and chemical bond compositions of g-C3N4, (001)-TiO2, and 80 C/CN/T were detected by the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 3(a). In g-C3N4, which contains a small number of hydrogenated N atoms [40], the N-H stretching vibration produces a broad band at 3153.25 cm−1; the elongation of the C-N bond is responsible for the absorption band at 1637.33 cm−1; the absorption bands at 1239.70, 1320.61, and 1407.99 cm−1 are ascribed to the extending of aromatic C-N; and the out-of-plane bending vibration of the tri-s-triazine unit leads to the absorption bands around 807.90 cm−1 [41–43]. Regarding the (001)-TiO2 spectra, the captured absorption bands at 3418.63, 1627.50, and 451.10 cm−1 are attributed to physically absorbing water molecules and surface hydroxyl stretching, surface hydroxyl bending, as well as Ti-O-Ti elongating [44], respectively. The vibration of the Ti-O-Ti and Ti-O-C bonds is closely related to the enlargement of the absorption band below 100 cm−1. The interaction between the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs and the hydroxyl group on the surface of (001)-TiO2 resulted in the creation of the Ti-O-C bond [45]. The 80 C/CN/T samples had the peaks for CN/T, evidencing the successful fusion of g-C3N4 and (001)-TiO2 and demonstrating that the CQDs had no direct effect on the infrared light absorption strength of CN/T.

The chemical structures and valence states of g-C3N4, (001)-TiO2, CN/T, and 80 C/CN/T can be deeper interpreted by XPS (Fig. 3(b–f)). The C signal at 288.38 eV and the N signal at 398.98 eV can be seen clearly in the g-C3N4 spectrum, as well as the Ti signal at 458.78 eV and the O signal at 529.98 eV can be captured in the pure (001)-TiO2 spectrum. The signals obtained from the composite samples for the elements C, N, Ti, and O are in excellent agreement with their respective compositions. It should be noted that the C1s peak of the 80 C/CN/T system is more potent than that of CN/T, which provides additional evidence that CQDs were implanted. As shown in Fig. 3(c), the C1s curve of 80 C/CN/T mainly fitted two binding energy peaks at 288.78 eV and 284.98 eV, which were attributed to the N-CN bond and the sp2-hybridized C atom of the C-C bond of g-C3N4 [46,47]. The N1s of 80 C/CN/T could be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 3(d)). The former one (407.08 eV) is likely put down to the N in the π-excitations, while the middle and latter (401.78 eV and 399.78 eV) are caused by the existence of the terminal amino group (C-N = H) and tertiary nitrogen (N-C3) [48,49]. It is worth pointing out that, compared with g-C3N4, the peaks of 80 C/CN/T exhibit a slight shift to the higher energy band, in which the density of the electron cloud surrounding the N element has decreased. The physical adsorption of water by TiO2 and the Ti-O-Ti link are all responsible for the two peaks at 532.08 eV and 530.18 eV in the O1s spectrum in Fig. 3(e) [40]. The two XPS peaks of Ti2p at 464.38 eV and 458.78 eV (Fig. 3(f)), which are the consequence of Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2 of TiO2 [37], offer proof of the presence of Ti4+. In agreement with the findings of the XRD, TEM, and FTIR analyses, the XPS results indicated that CQDs were contained in the composites.

The light absorption properties of samples were evaluated through the UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectroscopy measurements presented in Fig. 4(a). It is shown that (001)-TiO2 almost only absorbs ultraviolet light with λ < 410 nm, and the absorption edge of g-C3N4 is at 490 nm, however, its absorption intensity in the ultraviolet region is far below that of (001)-TiO2. The red-shift phenomenon in the spectral lines of CN/T is visible, and the absorption edge sits between them and closer to g-C3N4. As a result of the function of CQDs as photosensitizers [36], the optical absorption strength of 80 C/CN/T is increased, especially in the visible light range, and the absorption edge even exceeds that of g-C3N4, which is also one of the chief causes of higher visible light photocatalytic activity of the composite samples.

The energy bands (Eg) of the various samples were fitted by using the function tangent lines of Kubelka-Munk (Fig. 4(b)). The bandgap values of (001)-TiO2, g-C3N4, CN/T, and 80 C/CN/T have been calculated to be 2.80 eV, 2.44 eV, 2.39 eV, and 2.27 eV by Eq. (1) [50].
(1)
α h v
∙
1
n
= A ( hv − Eg )
Where α is the absorption index, h is Planck's constant, v is the frequency of incident light, A is the constant, and Eg is the semiconductor bandgap width. When the semiconductor has an indirect bandgap, n equals 2, and when it has a direct bandgap, n equals 1/2. The bandgap of (001)-TiO2 is significantly narrowed by the recombination of g-C3N4 with (001)-TiO2. CQDs are more prone to functionalization and defects on the catalyst surface due to their distinctive photoluminescence (PL) and up-conversion effects [51,52], which further improve the ability of samples to absorb visible light and the utilization rate of light.

The charge transfer process of the photo-induced electrons and holes was further verified by the transient photocurrent responses (Fig. 4(c)) and EIS. The light-on-light-off operation was carried out every 10 s. The rapid occurrence of photocurrent upon light activation demonstrates the enhanced photoresponse of the composite catalyst, potentially attributed to the minimal recombination of photogenerated carriers upon light cessation. At the same time, CQDs effectively capture electrons at the internal junction of the composite catalyst. Along with maintaining the dark state, the photocatalytic process is interrupted, no electrons are gathered by CQDs, and the carriers cannot separate. During this period, the resistance gradually increases, resulting in a corresponding decrease in the current intensity curve of C/CN/T upon switching off the light [53]. The intensity of transient photocurrent among the samples with the maximum value is 80 C/CN/T, which is about 2.5 times greater than CN/T. This phenomenon indicates an increase in the number of electrons traversing C/CN/T upon light activation, attributable to CQDs. When combined with Fig. 4(d), the semicircle diameter of the EIS spectra for C/CN/T is significantly smaller than that of (001)-TiO2 and CN/T, indicating a faster interface charge transfer rate for C/CN/T. This can be attributed to the strong chemical interaction between CQDs and (001)-TiO2 [38], which facilitates charge transfer between them. Meanwhile, as the electron acceptors, CQDs continuously capture and store photogenerated electrons [54], stimulating the separation of electron-hole pairs [55] and resulting in C/CN/T having a lower recombination rate between photogenerated carriers than CN/T. Consequently, C/CN/T displays a lower resistance than CN/T.

RhB and TC-HCl degradation experiments were performed under light illumination. Fig. 5(a) and (c) show that all samples nearly finished the degradation reaction in 30 min under UV light. 50 C/CN/T was the first sample to stop degrading under UV light, reaching 100 % efficiency in 30 min. Under visible light irradiation, 80 C/CN/T reached 96 % in 90 min and exceeded 99 % in 2 h. In the case of TC-HCl (Fig. 6(a), (c)), the degradation efficiency of 80 C/CN/T was 100 % only after being exposed to UV light for 10 min, and 91 % of the TC-HCl had disintegrated after being exposed to visible light for 70 min. The relative concentration (C/C0) is plotted as the functions of time (t) in Fig. 5(b), (d) and Fig. 6(b), (d), where C and C0 represent the residual and initial concentrations, respectively. The increased photocatalytic activity is attributable to the more excellent capability of the catalyst to collect visible light as well as the effective transmission and separation of photogenerated electrons and holes. These improvements depend on the addition of CQDs, which raises the visible light absorption power of heterojunction and promotes the photocatalytic performance of CN/T. The degrading efficiency of various catalysts is distinguished more markedly under visible light than under UV light. Insufficient CQDs may impede the up-conversion effect, hindering g-C3N4 from fully harnessing visible light. Conversely, excessive CQDs can cause significant light scattering [56] and disrupt the crystal structure of the tri-s-triazine unit [49], thereby reducing the utilization efficiency of visible light. The reusability is evaluated by recycling use of the 80 C/CN/T through four runs for the degradation of RhB and TC-HCl under visible light ( Fig. 7). The fact that the activity remained constant throughout the course of four cycles, and the degradation efficiency was only reduced with less than 0.2 % for RhB and 6.5 % for TC-HCl, implies that the photocatalyst has good cycling stability.

The trapping experiment was adopted in order to provide a qualitative analysis of the active species involved in photodegradation. Fig. 8(a) elucidates the results of using ammonium oxalate (AO), tert-butanol (TBA), and p-benzoquinone (P-BQ) as quenchers of photogenerated holes (h+), hydroxyl radicals (·OH), and superoxide radicals (·O2 -), respectively. While the degradation efficiency of RhB was sharply reduced from 99 % to 1.7 % under AO, which suggests that h+ played a major role in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB. The removal rate of RhB respectively decreased by 41.4 % and 55.8 % after the addition of TBA and P-BQ, implying that·OH and·O2 - radicals play a subordinate role in the photocatalytic reaction process. Therefore, the three reactive species of h+,·OH, and·O2 - collaborate during the photocatalytic reaction for 80 C/CN/T.

Based on the results of the active species capture test and other research [57,58], we endeavor to speculate on the catalytic degradation mechanism of C/CN/T. In a conventional type II heterojunction (Fig. 8(b)), the photogenerated electrons (e-) would be stimulated and migrate from the conduction band (CB) of g-C3N4 to that of (001)-TiO2, while the holes (h+) would move in the opposite direction along their respective transport paths [59,60]. Nevertheless, the h+ on VB cannot oxidize OH- and H2O because the position of VB of g-C3N4 (+1.50 eV) is lower than the redox potentials of typical·OH/OH- and·OH/H2O (+1.99 eV、+2.73 eV), hence h+ will interact directly with the contamination reaction while·OH radicals are not involved in the reaction [61–63]. But this result conflicts with the fact about the free radical trapping experiment. Following inferences, CN/T is assumed to be a Z-type heterojunction (Fig. 8(c)). Primarily, the up-conversion effect of CQDs enhances the performance of catalyst to utilize visible light by converting the near-infrared light from sunlight into visible light [64]. The e- is produced in the VB after the semiconductor materials are irradiated by visible light. Then e- migrates to the CB, and the corresponding amount of h+ generates on the VB. Then, a little h+ on the VB of g-C3N4 and the e- of CB of (001)-TiO2 are coupled. The e- of CB of g-C3N4 is adsorbed by CQDs, and the CQDs are equivalent to electron stores [48]. Subsequently, these e- are caught by O2 on the surface of catalysts and reduce O2 to·O2 - radicals. The h+ of VB of (001)-TiO2 oxidizes OH- and H2O to·OH radicals and simultaneously oxidizes the pollutants on the catalyst surface. In addition, when the catalyst adsorbed pollutant molecules, the aromatic rings and oxygen-containing groups of CQDs assisted the catalyst in accelerating the photo-induced dissolution of pollutant molecules through π-π conjugations [65]. At last, these active substances continue to react with RhB or TC-HCl to reach the goal of degradation.

In summary, this research successfully synthesized uniformly dense Z-type C/CN/T through a straightforward electrostatic self-assembly approach. We have confirmed that 80 C/CN/T exhibited excellent cycling stability and demonstrated superior catalytic activity towards RhB and TC-HCl under both UV and visible light irradiation. In the photocatalytic reaction, the active species h+,·OH, and·O2 - radicals all had contributions, and the h+ is the predominant active substance. The exceptional photocatalytic performance of TiO2 is attributed not only to the highly exposed (001) facet, which provides more reactive sites for the reaction but also to the distinctive up-conversion effect of CQDs that broadens the photoresponse scope and retards electron-hole pairs recombination. Moreover, the electron transport properties further enhance its photocatalytic performance. This work offers a fresh development concept for photocatalyst applications in the environment, particularly in wastewater treatment.

Yueying Wang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Writing. Xiaofeng Yang: Project administration. Xinwei Liu: Methodology, Investigation. Meidan Que: Conceptualization, Project administration. Yanjun Li: Project administration. Jin Chen: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Independent Research and Development project of State Key Laboratory of Green Building in Western China (No. LSZZ202118 to Jin Chen), the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 21JY021 to Xiaofeng Yang), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province (No. 2023-JC-YB-454 to Xiaofeng Yang), the State Key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy (Wuhan University of Science and Technology) (No. G202108 to Yanjun Li).
